nodes.label: Core Injection Runtime (QwenInjector, TriggerInjector, AutoInjector, markers)

edges.label: invokes run_* mains as GPU jobs
reuses Prefix machinery for KV construction

modal.text: modal_blends.py is pure glue, not a mechanism — one big file of Modal (a serverless GPU platform) @app.function wrappers that ship this repo's src/ and data/ to a remote GPU (A10G/A100/H100), forward CLI-style args into one of the run_*.py experiment scripts, and stream stdout back. It also defines the FastAPI chat endpoints (chat_app_7b, chat_app_a100_spot, etc.) that load a model plus a directory of saved AxiomMLP axioms via chat_server.preload/create_app, and functions to train and persist axioms to a Modal Volume via axiom_store.save_axiom. Nothing here is novel logic; it exists so experiments don't need a local GPU.

chat_server.text: The label bundles two unrelated things. src/marker/chat_server.py is the real production server: preload() loads a model plus every saved AxiomMLP (the trained MLP-adapter mechanism from run_axiom_mlp_demo.py) from disk, and create_app() exposes /chat via an AxiomSession that decides which axioms are 'active' per conversation. src/marker/chat.py is a separate, local-only CLI REPL that uses a completely different mechanism — it builds an AxiomPlan per axiom via axiom_plan.build_axiom_plan and drives AutoInjector's eop/steer/disambig vector injection instead of MLP adapters. They don't share a code path; treat them as two independent demos of two different axiom mechanisms.

injection_core.text: This is the actual injection engine that the whole project builds vectors for. run_injection.py's QwenInjector is a forward hook (a callback PyTorch runs during a layer's pass) that adds a vector into the residual stream at one position; trigger_inject.py's TriggerInjector is the real runtime path — it scans token ids for a registered axiom term and injects there automatically, KV-cache-aware so generation stays fast. auto_injector.py's AutoInjector generalizes this to many axioms and mechanisms (eop/steer/disambig) at once. markers.py is an older, narrower helper that wraps terms in '[[...]]' brackets so residuals can be captured at the closing marker — superseded by trigger_inject's token matching, which needs no visible markers; __init__.py itself is empty, just a package marker.

vector_extraction.text: run_extraction.py is an early experiment script (not a reusable library) that captures a 'meaning vector' at the closing '[[...]]' marker position and checks how distinct it is from neutral prose — the predecessor to the marker-free approach used everywhere else. vector_builder.py is the actual reusable piece: make_vector_builder returns a closure that builds each of the three vector kinds (eop/steer/disambig) axiom_plan.py needs, from a set of paraphrases and a live QwenInjector. run_n_axiom.py extends the same extraction idea to three concepts with contrastive subtraction — again an experiment script, not infrastructure. marker_replacement.py is a distinct idea: MarkerRegistry swaps a term like 'Balance Publisher' for an opaque made-up string ('ZQVXM0004') before the model ever sees it, removing lexical priors from that position entirely rather than fighting them with a vector.

axiom_planning.text: axiom_classifier.py is the decision logic: classify_lexical_prior() checks whether a term's word-pieces are common English words (the model would default to a wrong reading), and select_stack() picks which mechanisms to combine — eop always, +steer if the term's lexical prior is strong, +disambig only on smaller models where it was found to help rather than hurt. axiom_plan.py's build_axiom_plan() bundles the classifier's recommended stack with actual vectors from a caller-supplied builder into one AxiomPlan, the object AutoInjector consumes. axiom_registry.py is the single source of truth for every axiom used across the test suite (AXIOMS, CHAIN_AXIOMS, HIERARCHICAL_AXIOMS dicts of term/description/paraphrase-path), plus composed_description(), which concatenates a top-level axiom with its sub-axioms into one document for capturing compositional/DAG-shaped prefixes.

prefix_tuning.text: prefix_tuning.py's Prefix captures the model's actual attention K/V cache (its internal 'working memory' after reading a description) via Prefix.from_description, and can gradient-refine those K/V tensors afterward with train_prefix_contrastive — despite the frozen base model, this refinement step is a genuine train, not an understand, because the prefix's own numbers get updated by gradient descent. combined_cache() handles concatenating several axioms' prefixes with a RoPE (rotary position embedding) offset correction so they don't collide. prefix_poc.py is a separate, more radical variant: it trains N virtual K/V tokens completely from scratch against Q+A cross-entropy, with no grounding in real description text — this is the mechanism behind the recent 'crowding' capacity experiments.

soft_prompts.text: All three files here train an embedding-space vector (a per-axiom parameter that gets a gradient, not the model's own weights) to replace what the term's own tokens would normally look like. soft_prompt.py (v1) trains one vector per term sub-token; soft_prompt_plus.py adds extra trainable 'ghost' token slots right after the term for more capacity, and its v3-v8 training functions layer on cosine LR decay, batching, norm anchoring, and teacher-distilled synthetic Q+A. soft_prompt_slots.py (v9) is the latest iteration: it assigns one dedicated slot per fact and masks the gradient each step so only that fact's slot updates, aiming for a focused memory cell per fact rather than one blended vector.

slot_axiom.text: slot_axiom.py's SlotAxiom gives each axiom a disjoint, non-overlapping slice of the residual-stream's hidden dimensions at one chosen layer, and trains a vector (zero-initialized so it's a strict no-op until trained) that gets added only to that slice. It's a controlled test of whether the model's later layers can read information written into an arbitrary dimension range rather than at a token position — a different composition strategy from the token-position vectors used elsewhere.

axiom_mlp.text: run_axiom_mlp_demo.py is the project's current real per-axiom mechanism, and the one chat_server.py actually loads: a small trained MLP adapter (SmallMLP) at three chosen layers per axiom, trained on hand-written plus teacher-distilled synthetic Q+A plus boundary examples, paired with a frozen KV cache of the axiom's own description so fact retrieval isn't entirely dependent on what the tiny MLP memorized. run_axiom_mlp_mini.py is a scaled-down sanity check of the same idea against one fictional axiom ('Glorbox'), small enough to run on a laptop's CPU/MPS. axiom_store.py is thin glue — just save_axiom/load_axiom/load_all_axioms to serialize an AxiomMLP to and from a .pt file.

kv_methods.text: Both files try to shrink the per-axiom KV cache storage cost, and both are shared-encoder ideas trained once and reused. kv_compression.py's KVCompressor is a small MLP that compresses a full captured description KV down to a handful of virtual tokens per layer, trained end-to-end against answer cross-entropy. kv_hypernet.py's KVHypernet goes further: it only stores a tiny latent z per axiom plus the verbatim 'irreducible facts' text, and a shared decoder expands z back into a KV 'scaffold' at load time. Per the project's own journal, this hypernet approach was tried and killed — it didn't validate — so treat this file as a documented negative result, not a live mechanism.

token_identity.text: This family actually trains model parameters, unlike the injection-vector mechanisms elsewhere. seed_token.py adds one brand-new vocabulary token (e.g. '<Glorbox>') and trains only that new embedding row via a gradient mask that zeroes every other row's gradient, so the rest of the model is provably untouched. value_token.py extends the same trick to register verbatim value strings (like 'balances.raw') as their own single emittable tokens, sharing one grad mask with the seed token. bolt_selector.py's BoltSelector adds trained low-rank adapters at each layer that only fire when the seed token is present in the current input, and single_vector_store.py is glue that saves/loads the seed+bolt combo, mirroring axiom_store.py.

crowding.text: crowding.py is a synthetic data generator for the 'crowding' experiment referenced in the recent journal entries (prefix tuning validated, hypernet killed, crowding mapped): it defines a catalog of 38 attribute types (poll_interval, cache_ttl, etc.) with deterministic value samplers and a shared question-template family, so the team can generate axioms with an arbitrary number of facts (up to 32) instead of hand-writing paraphrases for each one. The goal is measuring how many facts fit into a single trained prefix before recall degrades — a capacity/scaling question, not a new mechanism.

paraphrase_expander.text: paraphrase_expander.py is a data-augmentation helper, not a mechanism: it few-shot prompts the model itself with existing paraphrases and asks it to generate more, then filters candidates for length, term presence, and duplicates. Used to cheaply grow a paraphrase set when hand-writing more would be too slow.

experiment_scripts.text: This is roughly 60 one-off run_*.py/probe_*.py scripts, each testing a single hypothesis or ablation with results in printed output/artifacts rather than a maintained API. run_combined_steering.py stacks three mechanisms (disambig+eop+steer) at three different layers on one axiom; run_iti_intervention.py implements ITI (Inference-Time Intervention, a technique from a different paper) which intervenes on specific attention heads instead of the residual stream; probe_full_patching.py is an activation-patching probe that swaps in 'correct' residuals position-by-position and layer-by-layer to localize where a wrong lexical reading lives; run_alpha_autotune.py auto-tunes injection strength (alpha) per axiom via held-out loss instead of manual guessing; run_axiom_battery.py runs the eop+contrastive mechanism across four axiom archetypes as a regression battery. The versioned filenames (v3 through v8, 'mini' vs 'demo') reflect incremental iteration kept for reproducibility, not live modules.

historical.text: Every file here is an explicitly marked negative or superseded result, kept for reproducibility rather than active use — all four techniques were attempts to make 3+ stacked axiom prefixes compose correctly, and all were beaten by the much simpler composed_description() approach in axiom_registry.py (concatenate the axioms into one document and capture a single prefix over it). ape.py (Adaptive Parallel Encoding) is marked 'superseded'; per_block_attention.py is marked 'confirmed dead end' by its own status note, reproducing a published paper's accuracy collapse; axiom_signatures.py adds a deterministic per-axiom fingerprint to K vectors to stop stacked axioms' identity vectors from colliding; selective_recompute.py (a CacheBlend-style approach) is marked as failing identically to the RoPE-fix concat it was meant to improve on. historical/__init__.py is empty, just a package marker.

tests.text: 25 test files plus a historical/ subdir, run with pytest. The style matches this project's convention for mech-interp work: tests pin mechanical invariants and pure-Python logic rather than numerical experiment outcomes. test_axiom_classifier.py directly asserts classify_lexical_prior() and select_stack()'s decisions (e.g. 'shoe_town' components are both common English words, so it must classify HIGH) with no model loaded; test_prefix_tuned.py checks data-hygiene invariants like train/dev/test paraphrase sets being pairwise disjoint. Actual experiment results (numbers, plots) are expected to live in artifacts, not assertions.

nodes.label: Load intended/lexical paraphrase sets for the term
Extract per-concept residual vectors (EOP / term-position) and build contrastive vectors
classify_lexical_prior(term): are the term's word-pieces common English?
select_stack(prior, complexity, model_layers): choose eop/steer/disambig mechanisms + layer + alpha
build_axiom_plan(): bundle mechanisms + vectors into AxiomPlan
AutoInjector/TriggerInjector.attach(): register one forward hook per mechanism layer
generate(prompt): run frozen model forward pass for next token
find_matches(current token ids, registry): term tokens present in this window?
Hook adds alpha * mechanism vector into residual at matched term positions
Take argmax token, append to sequence, continue via KV cache
Return generated text: frozen model now answers with the injected axiom meaning

load_paraphrases.text: Loads a term's example sentences ('paraphrases' - different wordings of the same definition) from a JSON file via load_paraphrases() in run_extraction.py, reused by run_injection.py. Despite the label, this only loads the intended-meaning set; the separate load_negatives() loads neutral 'negative' prose for contrast, and 'lexical' paraphrases (for disambig) are a distinct concept introduced later in vector_builder.py/chat.py.

extract_vectors.text: Converts paraphrases into vectors in the residual stream (the running sum of vectors flowing layer-to-layer through the model). vector_builder.py's _eop_vector averages the residual at each paraphrase's last token ('EOP' = end-of-paraphrase); _at_term_vector does the same at the term's own tokens. The label's 'contrastive vectors' refers to run_n_axiom.py's build_contrastive (a key minus the mean of other axioms' keys) - a standalone experiment, not something the live chat.py/auto_injector pipeline calls.

classify_prior.text: classify_lexical_prior(term) in axiom_classifier.py splits the term into words and checks each against a small hardcoded common-English-word list, returning LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH. This estimates the 'lexical prior' - how strongly the model's existing everyday reading of those words will resist the new meaning being injected.

select_stack.text: select_stack(prior, complexity, model_layers) in axiom_classifier.py chooses which mechanisms run: eop always, steer added when prior is medium/high, disambig added only when prior is high and the model has 26 or fewer layers (helps small models, hurts big ones per the project's experiment log - note Qwen 2.5 1.5B has 28 layers, so disambig never actually fires for it here). It also sets each mechanism's layer (as a fraction of model depth) and alpha (injection strength).

build_mech_vectors.text: vector_builder.make_vector_builder() returns a build(kind, layer) function; build_axiom_plan calls it once per chosen mechanism to compute the vector: eop = mean residual at paraphrase end, steer = a direction in vocabulary-logit space from the unembedding matrix, disambig = intended minus lexical-baseline at-term residual. run_bp_baseline_vs_injection.py is just a demo script exercising this, not core logic.

assemble_plan.text: build_axiom_plan() in axiom_plan.py bundles the classifier's recipe (lexical_prior, complexity, target_tokens) with the vectors just built into one AxiomPlan: term, term_variants, mechanisms {kind: layer/alpha/vector}, target_tokens. A mechanism missing required data (e.g. disambig with no lexical_baseline) is silently dropped rather than raising an error.

attach_injector.text: AutoInjector.attach() (auto_injector.py) registers one forward hook (a callback PyTorch runs during the model's forward pass) per distinct layer used across all plans, so one hook can serve several mechanisms or axioms sharing a layer. TriggerInjector.attach() (trigger_inject.py) is the older, simpler sibling: exactly one layer, one fixed alpha, built from a plain Registry rather than an AxiomPlan.

generate_step.text: generate(prompt) - duplicated in auto_injector.py and trigger_inject.py - tokenizes the prompt, runs one full forward pass for the first next-token scores, then decodes one token at a time reusing past_key_values (a cache of earlier attention keys/values, the 'KV cache', so the model never recomputes the whole prompt).

scan_match.text: find_matches(ids, registry) in trigger_inject.py scans the current token window for any registered term's tokenized form, checked both with and without a leading space since BPE (the sub-word tokenizer) encodes them differently depending on what precedes them. It keeps the longest, non-overlapping matches. Both TriggerInjector and AutoInjector's hooks call this same function.

inject_vector.text: When a match is found, the hook clones the transformer block's output and adds alpha times the mechanism's vector into the residual stream at every matched token position, cast to the residual's numeric precision first.

pass_through.text: If nothing matches in this forward pass (or alpha is 0), the hook returns the layer's output completely unchanged - the frozen model behaves exactly as if no injection code existed. This is the 'zero-vec inject is a no-op' invariant the project's tests are meant to pin down.

decode_next.text: The model's raw next-token scores ('logits', its pre-softmax scores over the vocabulary) are reduced to one choice by argmax - always the highest-scoring token, no randomness - appended to the sequence and fed back in next step alongside the KV cache.

eos_check.text: The loop stops early if the chosen token equals the tokenizer's EOS (end-of-sequence) id, or otherwise after max_new_tokens steps; if not finished, it loops back to generate_step for another token.

final_output.text: The full id sequence is decoded back to text and the original prompt is sliced off the front. No model weights changed anywhere in this process - only residual-stream values passing through at inference time were altered, which is why this is 'understand' (activation injection) rather than 'train'.

nodes.label: Prefix.from_description: one forward pass captures per-layer K/V cache
train_prefix_contrastive: gradient-refine K/V against intended vs lexical paraphrase NLL
combined_cache: concatenate per-axiom K/V, rotate K via rope offset
Encode composed_description jointly as one shared prefix
to_cache / build_prefix_cache: wrap K/V as DynamicCache
generate_with_prefixes: prepend cache as past_key_values, decode answer

desc.text: This is the plain-English description text for an axiom (a fact the model does not already know), stored in the AXIOMS/CHAIN_AXIOMS/HIERARCHICAL_AXIOMS dictionaries in axiom_registry.py. run_prefix_demo.py pulls this text out to feed to the model. Nothing clever happens yet — it is just the raw prose the model will read once.

buildprefix.text: Prefix.from_description in prefix_tuning.py runs the axiom description through the model once and grabs the KV cache — the key/value tensors the model builds at every layer while reading a text — that the model produces while reading it. Those tensors are truncated to a fixed token budget and stored as trainable parameters. This one forward pass is the seed for everything downstream; no gradient descent has happened yet.

trainq.text: A decision point (the --skip-training flag in run_prefix_demo.py, checked in prefix_tuning.py): do we further refine the captured K/V snapshot with gradient descent, or use it exactly as captured? Skipping is the validated default per the module docstring; training is an optional extra pass.

train.text: train_prefix_contrastive in prefix_tuning.py performs real gradient descent (backpropagation) directly on the captured K/V tensors — they are nn.Parameter objects, so calling .backward() and stepping an Adam optimizer changes those exact numbers in place. This is train in the precise sense: an actual weight-like update, not activation injection. It nudges the prefix toward the intended meaning and away from a lexically-similar wrong paraphrase, while an anchor loss keeps it near its description-based starting point.

hypernetq.text: Another decision point: instead of storing each axiom's full per-layer K/V tensors (about 5MB each, per the prefix_tuning.py docstring), should they be compressed through a shared hypernetwork defined in kv_hypernet.py? This trades a little fidelity for being able to store many axioms cheaply.

encode.text: KVHypernet.encode in kv_hypernet.py is a small neural network (a hypernetwork — a network whose job is to produce compact codes for other data) that pools an axiom's full K/V tensors per layer and squashes them into one small latent vector z. Producing z is a single forward pass with no training needed when a new axiom is registered.

decode.text: decode_scaffold in kv_hypernet.py runs z back through the shared decoder to reconstruct a small set of scaffold K/V tokens per layer — the generic, predictable prose structure. assemble_kv then concatenates that scaffold with the axiom's verbatim 'irreducible facts' K/V (exact details like numbers and names, which are never compressed) into one final injectable KV, with position rotations corrected so the two pieces line up.

chainq.text: A decision point in run_chain_demo.py and run_composed_axiom_demo.py: when a question needs facts from more than one axiom, how should their separately-captured prefixes be turned into one attention context? Two alternative strategies are tested here, not two steps of one pipeline — concatenate the separate prefixes (combine, next node) or re-encode all the descriptions together as one new prefix (jointenc, the node after that).

combine.text: combined_cache in prefix_tuning.py is one of the two alternative ways to combine axioms: it concatenates several axioms' already-captured K/V prefixes layer by layer. Because each prefix was originally captured as if it started at position 0, combined_cache re-applies RoPE (rotary position embedding, how the model encodes a token's position into its attention math) to shift each later prefix's keys to its true position in the combined sequence, so the model can tell the prefixes apart.

jointenc.text: The other alternative combination strategy, used in run_composed_axiom_demo.py: composed_description in axiom_registry.py stitches the top-level axiom text, each sub-axiom's description, and a how-it-fits-together note into one document, which is then captured as a single shared prefix via Prefix.from_description. This lets the model's own attention build the cross-axiom connections while reading once, instead of gluing separately-read prefixes together afterward.

tocache.text: Prefix.to_cache in prefix_tuning.py (and the proof-of-concept variant build_prefix_cache in prefix_poc.py) packages whatever K/V tensors were produced — a plain prefix, a combined multi-axiom prefix, or a hypernetwork-decoded one — into a DynamicCache, the standard object the transformers library uses to hold cached attention state. This step is just a format conversion so the model can accept it as a starting cache.

generate.text: generate_with_prefixes in prefix_tuning.py runs the actual question through the model with the prepared cache passed in as past_key_values (the model's starting attention memory), then decodes the answer one token at a time. Because the axiom's K/V is already sitting in the cache, the model attends to it at every layer without ever re-reading the description text.

nodes.label: Init soft-prompt vector from term's BPE embeddings + n_ghost extra rows (SoftPromptPlus.from_term)
Init ghost rows from description's post-term tokens (from_term_with_context)
Generate synthetic Q+A pairs via teacher model + full description prefix (generate_synthetic_qa_pairs)
Assemble training set: synthetic + supplemental + boundary + overview Q+A pairs
Sample Q+A pair(s) for this step
Tokenize Q+A text, insert n_ghost pad tokens right after term's last sub-token (prepare_input_with_ghosts)
Drop pair (no positions to train on)
Mask question tokens with -100, keep only answer (+EOS) tokens as labels
Install embedding forward hook substituting term+ghost positions with sp.vector rows
Forward pass through frozen model, compute LM loss on answer tokens
Add norm-anchor regularization to natural embedding L2 norm?
Backward + AdamW step on sp.vector only, cosine LR decay
Generate with trained soft prompt at term+ghost positions (generate_with_soft_prompt_plus)
More versions (v3–v8) or slot variant to run?
Slots demo: assign one Q+A role per ghost slot, gradient-mask updates so only the active slot trains per step (train_soft_prompt_slots)
Axiom recitation validated across TRAIN/HELDOUT/BOUNDARY/TELL_ME probes

init_vec.text: SoftPromptPlus.from_term builds the trainable vector: it copies the term's real embeddings for each of its BPE tokens, then appends n_ghost extra rows of zeros as blank ghost token slots to be filled in by training. This vector, not the model, is what gets trained — everything else stays frozen.

ctx_init.text: Decision point: does this run use the plain from_term init or the context-aware from_term_with_context init? The v8 demo (run_soft_prompt_plus_v8_demo.py) is the one that opts into context init; earlier versions leave the ghost rows at zero.

ctx_init_yes.text: SoftPromptPlus.from_term_with_context fills the ghost rows from the description's own tokens instead of zeros: it finds where the term appears in the description and copies the embeddings of the words that follow it there. Any ghost slots left over (description too short) fall back to zero, same as the plain init.

gen_synth_qa.text: generate_synthetic_qa_pairs prompts the model itself (with the full KV-cache prefix loaded as a 'teacher') to write question-and-answer pairs covering the description, then regex-parses its output into (question, answer) tuples. This is only used by the v8 demo (run_soft_prompt_plus_v8_demo.py) to bulk up the training set without hand-writing every Q+A pair.

build_train_set.text: Each demo script assembles its own training set before calling the trainer: v4's demo combines hand-written fact Q+A pairs with generated 'doesn't specify...' boundary examples and a few 'Tell me about X' overview prompts, while v8's demo instead uses the synthetic Q+A pairs from the previous step (replicated) plus the same boundary and overview pairs.

sample_batch.text: At each training step the trainer (e.g. train_soft_prompt_plus_qa_v4 or the batched train_soft_prompt_plus_qa_v6_batched) randomly samples one Q+A pair — or several for the batched version — from the assembled set to train on this step.

expand_ghosts.text: prepare_input_with_ghosts tokenizes the 'Q: ...\nA: ...' text into BPE tokens, finds the term's last sub-token, and splices in n_ghost placeholder pad tokens right after it. It returns both the expanded token sequence and the list of positions (term tokens plus ghost tokens) that training will later overwrite.

term_found.text: Decision inside prepare_input_with_ghosts: if the term's tokens can't be located in this particular Q+A text, there's nowhere to insert ghosts or substitute the trained vector, so it returns an empty positions list instead.

skip_pair.text: When a Q+A pair yields no positions (term not found), the training loops (e.g. train_soft_prompt_plus_qa_v4) just skip it with a continue — that pair contributes no gradient this run.

mask_labels.text: For pairs that do contain the term, the trainer marks every question token's label as -100 (PyTorch's 'ignore this token' sentinel) so only the answer tokens, plus an appended end-of-sequence token, count toward the LM loss (the standard next-token-prediction loss).

install_hook.text: install_soft_prompt_plus_hook registers a PyTorch forward hook on the model's embedding layer that, on this forward pass, overwrites the output rows at the term+ghost positions with the current values of sp.vector — this is the actual mechanism that injects the trainable rows into the model's input.

forward_loss.text: With the hook active, the trainer runs a normal forward pass (model(input_ids, labels=labels)) through the otherwise-frozen model; every parameter except sp.vector has requires_grad set to False, so the returned out.loss is the LM loss computed only on the answer tokens.

norm_anchor.text: Decision baked into which trainer function is called: v5 and later (train_soft_prompt_plus_qa_v5, train_soft_prompt_plus_qa_v6_batched) add a norm-anchor penalty; the original v4 trainer does not.

add_norm_loss.text: When norm-anchoring is on, the trainer computes each row of sp.vector's L2 norm (vector length), penalizes it for straying from the model vocabulary's average embedding norm, and adds that penalty (scaled by norm_anchor_lambda) to the model loss to get the total loss that gets backpropagated.

backward_step.text: total.backward() computes gradients, then a single AdamW optimizer step updates only sp.vector (every other parameter is frozen); the learning rate follows a cosine decay schedule from lr_start down to lr_end across all n_steps.

remove_hook.text: The embedding hook is removed (handle.remove()) in a finally block right after the optimizer step, so it doesn't linger and affect any other forward pass — e.g. the probing generations later install their own fresh copy of the same hook.

steps_left.text: Decision: has the loop hit its configured n_steps yet? If not, control loops back to the sample_batch step to draw a new Q+A pair and repeat; this loop is the entire training run (thousands of iterations, e.g. 3500 steps for v4 by default).

probe.text: generate_with_soft_prompt_plus reinstalls the same substitution hook and greedily generates a response token by token, so we can see what the model says once the trained ghost vectors are in place — this is inference, not training, and the model's weights are untouched.

next_version.text: Decision: each run_soft_prompt_plus_v*_demo.py script (v4 through v8) tries one variant of the recipe — more steps, batching, norm anchoring, synthetic data, context init — and soft_prompt_slots.py's run_soft_prompt_slots_demo.py branches off entirely into the slot-based variant.

slots_variant.text: train_soft_prompt_slots (soft_prompt_slots.py) is a different training scheme: each ghost position is pre-assigned to one specific Q+A pair ('slot'), and after backpropagating the loss for the sampled slot's question, the code zeroes out the gradient everywhere except that slot's row (and optionally the term rows) before the optimizer step, so each slot specializes on its own fact.

done.text: The demo scripts validate the trained soft prompt by running four probe sets — TRAIN (questions seen during training), HELDOUT (paraphrased questions never trained on), BOUNDARY (out-of-scope questions the description doesn't answer), and TELL_ME (open-ended 'describe X' prompts) — comparing the soft-prompt output against a no-axiom baseline and the full-prefix upper bound.

diagrams.name: Axiom MLP Adapter Training & Chat Serving

nodes.label: Load frozen base model + tokenizer for training
make_axiom_mlp(): create per-axiom MLP adapters at chosen layers
compute_axiom_kv(): build compact KV snippet from description
train(): fit MLP adapters + KV on Q+A / boundary pairs
save_axiom(): persist weights + KV + dependencies to .pt
preload(): load model + load_axiom() for every .pt in axiom_dir
Match axiom terms present in the message
_inject_kvs(terms): merge KV caches for active + dependency axioms
install_hooks(): register per-layer MLP forward hooks for active axioms
generate_with_mlps(): decode reply conditioned on injected KV + hooks
Return response + sorted active_axioms to client

load_model.text: Loads the base language model and its tokenizer, frozen (no weight updates), so it can be reused as the fixed 'brain' every axiom trains against.

build_prefix.text: Prefix.from_description runs the axiom's description text through the model once and keeps the resulting per-layer KV cache (key/value tensors) as a 'teacher' prefix — this is what lets the model answer as if it already read the description.

synth_qa.text: generate_synthetic_qa_pairs asks the model, primed with the teacher prefix, to invent its own Q+A pairs about the axiom so there is more than just the hand-written training data.

make_mlp.text: make_axiom_mlp() builds a small trainable MLP adapter (SmallMLP, a tiny down-project/up-project pair) at each of 3 chosen layers for this axiom — these are the only weights that get trained.

compute_kv.text: compute_axiom_kv() runs the plain description text through the frozen model once and freezes the resulting KV cache — this gets prepended at inference so the model can always 'see' the description, taking pressure off the MLP to memorize facts.

train_mlp.text: train() is the actual gradient-descent step: it fits the small MLP adapters (plus this frozen KV) against the Q+A pairs and boundary examples using AdamW, while every other model weight stays frozen.

save_axiom.text: save_axiom() writes the trained MLP weights, the frozen KV tensors, and metadata (dependencies, skill_mode) to a single .pt file on disk so this axiom never needs retraining.

more_axioms.text: Loop point: if there are more facts or skills to teach the model, go back and repeat the whole build-prefix-through-train cycle for the next axiom.

preload.text: preload() runs once when the chat server boots: it loads the frozen model, then calls load_axiom() on every .pt file in the axiom directory to rebuild all the trained AxiomMLP objects in memory.

create_app.text: create_app() wires up the FastAPI web server and mounts the /chat, /axioms, and /health HTTP routes clients will talk to.

chat_request.text: A user's HTTP POST to /chat arrives carrying the message text and an optional session_id to continue a prior conversation.

session_exists.text: Decision: has this session_id been seen before? If yes, reuse its existing AxiomSession (and its accumulated KV cache); if no, create a fresh one.

new_session.text: A new AxiomSession is created holding a reference to the shared model and the full list of loaded axiom adapters — nothing is injected yet, that happens lazily per turn.

session_chat.text: session.chat() is the per-turn entry point: it tokenizes the message and drives detection, dependency resolution, KV injection, hook installation, and generation for this one turn.

detect_terms.text: The session scans the incoming message's token ids for any axiom's term (e.g. 'BalancePublisher') appearing verbatim, to decide which axioms are relevant to this turn.

resolve_deps.text: _resolve_dependencies() walks an axiom's declared dependencies (e.g. MeshPublisher depends on ServiceProcess) recursively so a mention of a child term also pulls in everything it inherits from.

deps_found.text: Loop point: each dependency axiom found may itself have further dependencies, so resolution keeps recursing until no new axioms are discovered.

inject_kvs.text: _inject_kvs() merges the frozen description KV caches of every newly-active axiom (plus its dependencies) and appends them onto the tail of the session's running KV cache, RoPE-corrected so their position encodings line up correctly.

install_hooks.text: install_hooks() registers a PyTorch forward hook on each chosen transformer layer for every active axiom's MLP, so the adapter's output gets added into the residual stream (the running sum of vectors flowing layer-to-layer) exactly at that axiom's term position.

generate.text: generate_with_mlps() runs greedy decoding: the model produces its reply token by token while the injected KV cache and the installed MLP hooks both stay active, steering the output toward the trained facts/behavior.

return_response.text: The server sends back the generated text plus the sorted list of axioms that were active this turn, so the client can show what knowledge was in play.

reset_session.text: DELETE /session/{id} calls session.reset() to clear the accumulated KV cache and active-axiom state, then drops the session from the server's registry — the next message on that id starts a clean conversation.

nodes.label: Tokenize axiom term into original BPE ids
New token id >= current embedding rows?
Resize embedding table to fit new token
Token re-encodes as exactly one id?
Init new embedding row = mean of original BPE piece embeddings
Install backward hook zeroing all embedding-grad rows except the seed row
Build low-rank bolt-on adapter per layer, keyed to seed token
Install embedding pre-hook + per-layer forward hooks for the bolt
Jointly train seed embedding row + bolt adapter params on Q+A pairs
Seed token id present in this forward pass's input ids?
Per-layer hook adds adapter output into the residual stream
Persist trained seed embedding + bolt adapter to disk
Inference: generate with bolt hooks installed

tokenize.text: Splits the axiom term into its original sub-word pieces (BPE tokens, the tokenizer's usual vocabulary units) using the private helper _term_token_ids in seed_token.py; if the term produces no ids it retries with a leading space, since some tokenizers need that to match a piece.

addtoken.text: register_seed_token in seed_token.py adds a brand new entry, like '<BalancePublisher>', to the tokenizer's vocabulary (a vocab token is one sub-word unit the tokenizer knows) via AddedToken, giving the axiom's name its own dedicated token id.

resize.text: register_seed_token checks whether the new token's id falls outside the model's current embedding table (the embedding table is the matrix holding one row of numbers per vocab token, looked up before layer 1 runs). Some models like Qwen 2.5 pad extra rows already, so this avoids resizing when unnecessary.

doresize.text: When the new id doesn't fit, register_seed_token calls model.resize_token_embeddings(token_id + 1) to grow the embedding table by one row so the new token has somewhere to live.

verify.text: register_seed_token re-encodes the '<name>' string and checks it comes back as exactly the one new token id, since an added token could in theory still get split into multiple pieces.

raise.text: If re-encoding didn't collapse to a single id, register_seed_token raises a RuntimeError and abandons registration entirely, rather than silently training on a broken token.

initrow.text: register_seed_token fills the new embedding row with the average of the original BPE pieces' embedding vectors from step 1, giving the new token a sensible starting point instead of random noise.

freeze.text: _freeze_model_params in seed_token.py sets requires_grad=False on every parameter in the model, meaning nothing about the pretrained model can change during training except what gets explicitly re-enabled next.

gradmask.text: _install_seed_grad_mask re-enables gradients on the embedding table as a whole, then attaches a gradient hook (a callback that intercepts and edits gradients during backpropagation) that zeroes every row's gradient except the new seed token's row, so only that one row of numbers ever updates.

makebolt.text: make_bolt_selector in bolt_selector.py builds one small low-rank adapter (a compact pair of matrices, down-project then GELU then up-project, cheaper to train than a full layer) per transformer layer, with the up-projection zero-initialized so the adapter starts as a complete no-op.

installhooks.text: install_bolt_hooks registers a pre-hook on the embedding lookup plus one forward hook per transformer layer, wiring the adapters into the model without touching its actual weights.

train.text: train_seed_and_bolt jointly optimizes the seed embedding row and all the bolt adapters' parameters together, using cross-entropy loss computed only on the answer tokens of each Q+A pair; everything else stays frozen thanks to the freeze+gradmask steps above.

seedcheck.text: The embedding pre-hook installed above checks whether the seed token's id appears in the current forward pass's input ids and sets a 'fire' flag on the BoltSelector accordingly; in skill mode it also latches the flag true across single-token decode steps so a multi-word answer keeps the adapters engaged.

applyadapter.text: When the fire flag is set, each layer's forward hook runs that layer's adapter on the hidden states and adds the result straight into the residual stream (the running sum of vectors passed from layer to layer), nudging the model's behavior only when the seed token is present.

noop.text: When the fire flag is false, the same hooks just return the layer's output unchanged, so a normal prompt without the seed token flows through the frozen base model exactly as if the adapters didn't exist.

save.text: save_single_vector_axiom in single_vector_store.py writes the trained seed embedding vector, the term's original BPE ids, and the bolt adapters' state dict to a single .pt file, so the whole axiom can be reloaded later without retraining.

generate.text: generate_with_bolt in run_single_vector_demo.py installs the bolt hooks and calls the model's normal greedy generate loop; each new token it produces re-enters the model as input, so the process loops back to the seedcheck gate (step 13) at every decode step before the hooks are removed.

nodes.label: Build INTENDED-meaning and LEXICAL-meaning prompts sharing the same suffix
Forward-pass both prompts with output_hidden_states=True, capture last-position residual at every layer
Select single-BPE-token intended-vocab and lexical-vocab target ids
Compute baseline mean logit scores (intended vs lexical targets) on the unpatched lexical pass
Pick next layer L to test
Hook layer L: swap lexical pass's last-position residual with intended pass's residual at L
Re-run lexical prompt through the model with the patch hook active
Compute shift-toward-intended = patched (intended-lexical score) minus baseline diff
Also sweep positions (not just last token)?
Layer × position grid: patch intended residual at position k for each candidate layer, measure logit shift
Project residual through unembedding (logit lens) to sanity-check what each layer/position 'says'
best = layer (and position) with maximum causal shift toward intended meaning
Hot-spot layer/position becomes the target for injection pipelines (steering vectors, prefixes, axiom plans)

buildContrastPrompts.text: probe_activation_patching.py defines two hard-coded prompt strings, INTENDED_PREFIX and LEXICAL_PREFIX, each followed by the identical suffix ' Balance Publisher's main role is' so both prompts end on the same tokens. One prefix describes a trading-system tool, the other an accounting tool, letting the probe isolate which meaning the model leans on at that shared ending.

captureHiddenStates.text: main() runs qwen.model(...) with output_hidden_states=True on both the INTENDED and LEXICAL token sequences. That single call returns the residual stream (the running sum of vectors flowing layer to layer through the transformer) at the last token position for every layer at once.

defineTargetTokens.text: _single_token_ids() filters the hand-picked INTENDED_TARGET_TOKENS and LEXICAL_TARGET_TOKENS word lists (e.g. ' polling', ' exchange' vs ' accounting', ' financial') down to only words that tokenize to exactly one BPE token. This keeps later logit comparisons apples-to-apples on single vocabulary entries.

computeBaselineScores.text: _mean_logit() averages the model's pre-softmax scores (logits) for the intended-vocab and lexical-vocab token ids on the un-patched LEXICAL forward pass. This gives a 'before' reading of how lexical- vs intended-leaning the model already is, before any intervention.

selectLayer.text: The for layer in range(n_layers) loop in main() walks through every transformer layer one at a time so the patch-and-measure procedure (steps 6-8) can repeat at each depth. It loops back here after each layer until all layers are covered (see moreLayersDecision).

patchResidual.text: make_hook() registers a forward hook on layers_module[layer] that overwrites the LEXICAL pass's last-token residual with a saved copy of the INTENDED pass's residual at that same layer. This is activation patching: swapping one prompt's internal activation into another prompt's forward pass to test causal effect.

runPatchedForward.text: qwen.model(lexical_ids) is called again with the patch hook attached, forcing the LEXICAL prompt through a full forward pass while its residual at the chosen layer has secretly been replaced. The hook is removed afterward in a finally block so it doesn't leak into the next layer's test.

computeLayerShift.text: The script computes shift = (intended_score - lexical_score) minus that same difference from the un-patched baseline. That single number per layer measures how far the patch pushed the model's next-token logit toward the intended-meaning vocabulary.

moreLayersDecision.text: This is the loop-back point of the range(n_layers) for-loop in main(): after scoring one layer, control returns to selectLayer for the next index. Once every layer 0..n_layers-1 has been probed, the loop exits to identifyHotspotLayer.

positionSweepDecision.text: probe_gemma_patching.py and probe_full_patching_shoe_town.py extend the single-position idea with a --max-positions argument. Instead of patching only the very last token, they sweep the last K token positions too, since the term's own tokens (not just the final word) might be where meaning is anchored.

patchPositionResidual.text: Both scripts nest a loop over each candidate layer and each reverse-offset k, patching the INTENDED residual at position k into the LEXICAL pass at that layer via a forward hook (same make_hook pattern as patchResidual). They print a layer x position grid of logit shifts, revealing whether meaning lives at one point or is spread across several token positions.

logitLensCheck.text: probe_disambig_locus.py's logit_lens() takes a captured residual, runs it through the model's own final normalization and unembedding matrix (the logit lens: projecting an intermediate residual vector through the final unembedding matrix to see what token it 'looks like'), and reports the top predicted tokens. That gives a human-readable sanity check on what a given layer/position is 'thinking'.

identifyHotspotLayer.text: probe_activation_patching.py picks best = max(results, key=lambda r: r.delta), the layer whose patch produced the single largest shift toward intended-meaning tokens. The shoe_town and full-patching variants report the same kind of best (layer, position) pair from their 2-D grids, e.g. 'hot-spot at k=0 layer 26'.

guideInjectionTarget.text: Checked this carefully: it is NOT actually wired up. axiom_plan.py's build_axiom_plan() picks its injection layer via axiom_classifier.py's _layer_at_fraction(), a fixed heuristic based on model depth and a lexical-prior classification (e.g. 0.71/0.89/0.33 of total layers), and run_injection.py's QwenInjector takes --layer as a plain CLI argument (default 10). Neither reads any file or value produced by these probe scripts, so the link is manual: a human reads a probe's printed report (e.g. 'hot-spot at layer 26') and hand-copies that number elsewhere, which is presumably why run_logit_bias_decode.py's --layer default happens to be 26.

nodes.label: Paraphrase corpora (per-axiom JSON, loaded in chat runtime)
vector_builder: build eop/steer/disambig vectors from paraphrases + hidden states
axiom_plan: build_axiom_plan combines recipe + vectors into AxiomPlan
Registry: term token-id variants + meaning vector
TriggerInjector: single-layer hook, find_matches + inject at term positions
AutoInjector: multi-layer hooks over AxiomPlan mechanisms
Model forward pass / generate(): steered token output

edges.label: injected vector at matched token positions
per-layer injected vectors at matched token positions

paraphrases.text: Per-axiom JSON files under data/ (e.g. balance_publisher_paraphrases.json), each holding a 'positives' list of differently-worded example sentences, loaded by chat.py's _load(). Some axioms (shoe_town) also have a lexical-baseline file used only by the disambig mechanism - this is the raw text everything downstream is built from.

qwen.text: QwenInjector (run_injection.py) loads Qwen 2.5 1.5B and its tokenizer, and exposes hidden_states()/log_probs_at_last() for reading residual-stream activations. It also carries its own single-vector forward hook from the earlier marker-based experiments, but in the chat.py/AutoInjector pipeline it mainly acts as the model+tokenizer holder.

classifier.text: axiom_classifier.py's classify_lexical_prior / describe_axiom / select_stack decide, per term, whether its word-pieces are common English (the 'lexical prior' the injected meaning has to compete with), then pick which mechanisms (eop/steer/disambig) to run and at what layer/alpha. Pure decision logic - no model forward passes here.

vector_builder.text: vector_builder.py's make_vector_builder returns a build(kind, layer) closure that computes eop (mean end-of-paraphrase residual), steer (a logit-space direction from the unembedding matrix), or disambig (intended-minus-lexical at-term residual) vectors on demand.

axiom_plan.text: axiom_plan.py's build_axiom_plan runs describe_axiom then calls vector_builder.build for each recommended mechanism, packaging the result into one AxiomPlan - the single object the runtime needs per axiom, holding term variants and each mechanism's layer/alpha/vector.

registry.text: trigger_inject.py's Registry/Entry stores each axiom's term as its tokenized id variants (with and without a leading space) plus one meaning vector. TriggerInjector uses a real Registry built up-front; AutoInjector instead builds disposable one-term scratch registries inside its hook, purely to reuse the same find_matches() scanning logic.

trigger_injector.text: TriggerInjector (trigger_inject.py) is the older, single-mechanism runtime component: one layer, one fixed alpha, one Registry. Its forward hook calls find_matches() on the current token window and adds alpha times the vector at any matched term position.

auto_injector.text: AutoInjector (auto_injector.py) is the multi-mechanism runtime chat.py actually uses: it groups every plan's mechanisms by layer and registers one hook per distinct layer, so one axiom can inject at several layers (eop/steer/disambig) and several axioms can share a layer's hook.

chat_runtime.text: chat.py's build_all_plans() builds an AxiomPlan for every axiom in its AXIOMS list, via a slightly awkward two-pass process since the steer mechanism's target tokens aren't known until describe_axiom runs, so the vector builder gets rebuilt afterward. run_chat() then wires all plans into one AutoInjector and drives a plain input()-based REPL.

steered_generation.text: The generate() decode loop (duplicated in trigger_inject.py and auto_injector.py) is where injection shows up in output: greedy token-by-token generation with a KV cache, where the hook's residual-stream edits at matched positions change which tokens the frozen model picks next - no weights are ever updated.

edges.label: trained seed vector + bolt adapter weights

descriptions.text: The starting input for every mechanism below: a hand-written description and set of facts about a fictional or real subject (e.g. BalancePublisher), defined in TEST_AXIOMS, axiom_registry.py, or generated synthetically by crowding.make_axiom() for scale experiments.

handwritten_qa.text: Hand-written question/answer pairs per fact, used as the seed training data before any synthetic augmentation — shared raw material for all four mechanisms below.

teacher_prefix.text: Prefix.from_description builds a full, uncompressed KV cache (key/value tensors) from the description text — this acts as a 'teacher' the model can consult when generating its own training examples. Feeds only the MLP-adapter track.

synthetic_qa.text: generate_synthetic_qa_pairs uses the model, primed with the teacher prefix, to generate extra Q+A pairs by distillation (teacher-generated data the student adapter then learns from).

compute_kv.text: compute_axiom_kv freezes a compact KV cache of the description alone (no teacher prefix needed) — this is the KV that actually ships with the trained MLP adapter and gets injected at inference time.

mlp_train.text: make_axiom_mlp + train(): this is the ONE mechanism that is actually live in production — a small per-layer MLP adapter trained by gradient descent against the Q+A data plus the frozen description KV.

modal_pipeline.text: run_save_axioms in modal_blends.py is the Modal cloud orchestration script that runs this whole MLP-adapter training loop on a GPU for every axiom and commits the results to a persistent volume.

axiom_store.text: axiom_store.py's save_axiom/load_axiom/load_all_axioms is the on-disk format for the MLP-adapter mechanism — this is the ONLY one of the four storage formats on this diagram that chat_server.py actually reads.

seed_register.text: register_seed_token (in seed_token.py) is a DIFFERENT, alternative experiment: it adds a single new vocabulary token representing the subject's identity, instead of training an MLP adapter.

bolt_train.text: make_bolt_selector + train_seed_and_bolt (bolt_selector.py) trains a small 'bolt-on' adapter that routes from the seed token to the right answer tokens — a parked alternative to the MLP-adapter approach, not stages of the same pipeline.

single_vector_store.text: single_vector_store.py saves/loads this seed+bolt combination to its own .pt format, mirroring axiom_store.py but for the seed/bolt experiment — nothing in chat_server.py loads from here.

value_token_train.text: register_axiom_tokens + train_axiom_tokens (value_token.py) is a third, separate experiment: it promotes whole novel strings (like 'balances.raw') to single new vocabulary tokens so the model can emit them in one shot, trained alongside a bolt selector.

value_token_checkpoint.text: run_value_token_demo.py's ad-hoc _save() function is a bespoke checkpoint writer for the value-token experiment only — it is not a shared, reusable store like axiom_store.py, and chat_server.py never reads it.

hypernet_train.text: make_axiom_code + train_hypernet (kv_hypernet.py) is a fourth, separate experiment: a shared 'hypernetwork' (a network trained once to compress/expand many axioms) learns to compress each axiom's full KV cache into a small latent code and expand it back later.

hypernet_store.text: kv_hypernet.py's save/load_axiom_code and save/load_hypernet persist the tiny latent codes and the shared decoder network — again a separate format that chat_server.py does not touch.

chat_server.text: chat_server.py's preload() calls axiom_store.load_axiom() at startup — confirmed by reading the code, this is the only one of the four mechanisms actually wired into the serving path; the seed/bolt, value-token, and hypernet tracks remain parked experiments.

axiom_session.text: AxiomSession.chat (run_axiom_mlp_demo.py) is where all four tracks would converge if they were all live, but today it only ever runs on axioms loaded via the MLP-adapter path — this is the reuse-without-retraining point of the pipeline.

nodes.label: preload() — load model, tokenizer, axiom set
model.generate with injected axiom KV cache

deploy.text: Modal's chat app factory function boots the server, choosing which model + axiom set to load for this deployment (e.g. the 32B vs 7B endpoint).

preload.text: preload() runs once at startup: it loads the model, tokenizer, and every saved axiom before any HTTP request is served.

axiomDisk.text: The trained axiom .pt files live on a persistent disk/volume, written earlier by the training pipeline.

loadAxioms.text: load_all_axioms() reads every .pt file in the axiom directory and rebuilds each one as an in-memory AxiomMLP object.

serverState.text: The loaded model, tokenizer, and axiom list are kept as simple in-memory globals that every incoming request shares.

httpRequest.text: A client sends an HTTP POST to /chat with a message and optional session_id.

chatHandler.text: The /chat handler reads the request body and looks up or creates the session for this session_id.

sessionStore.text: A dict of session_id to AxiomSession object, so each conversation keeps its own accumulated KV cache and active-axiom state.

axiomSession.text: AxiomSession detects which axiom terms appear in the message and resolves their transitive dependencies before responding.

kvInjection.text: Newly-active axioms' frozen KV caches are merged and appended (RoPE-corrected) onto the session's running KV cache.

modelGenerate.text: model.generate decodes the reply with both the injected KV cache and the axiom MLP hooks active, steering the output toward trained facts/behavior.

chatResponse.text: The generated text and updated session/active-axiom info are sent back to the client as JSON.

nodes.label: Local entrypoints (main, probe, gauntlet, save_axioms, etc.)
Remote GPU functions (run_blends, run_combined, run_prefix, run_axiom_mlp_demo, run_save_axioms, ...)
Imported training/demo script main() (run_blends, run_combined_demo, run_prefix_demo, run_axiom_mlp_demo, register_axiom, probe_*, ...)

edges.label: CLI parameters (model_name, axiom, layers, n_steps, alphas) via .remote()
function return value over Modal RPC

localEntry.text: These are functions decorated with @app.local_entrypoint() that run on your own laptop when you type 'modal run modal_blends.py::something'. They do not touch the GPU themselves — their job is to package up your command-line arguments and hand them to a remote function (a Python function that actually executes on Modal's cloud GPU, invoked from your laptop via .remote()). Think of them as the front desk: you talk to them locally, they dispatch the real work elsewhere.

remoteFn.text: Each of these is decorated with @app.function(gpu=...) and is the code that actually runs on a rented cloud GPU, not your laptop. When a local entrypoint calls something like run_blends.remote(...), Modal spins up a container on that GPU, executes this function's body there, and sends back whatever it returns. There are roughly 20 of these, one per experiment variant such as blends, combined, prefix, and axiom_mlp.

hfCache.text: This is a Modal Volume (a persistent cloud disk Modal mounts into remote functions) named 'mimir-hf-cache', mounted at /root/.cache/huggingface inside every GPU function. Downloading a model like Qwen2.5-32B from Hugging Face the first time can take many minutes, so this volume caches those weights so every later run reuses them instead of re-downloading.

modelLoad.text: Once a remote function is running on the GPU, it calls Hugging Face's AutoModelForCausalLM and AutoTokenizer to load the language model's weights and its tokenizer (the component that turns text into the numeric tokens the model works with) into GPU memory. This step reads straight from the hfCache volume when the weights are already cached, which is what makes repeat runs start much faster.

scriptMain.text: Rather than duplicating experiment logic, each remote function fakes a command line (builds a sys.argv list) and imports the real experiment script's main() function directly, e.g. 'from marker.run_blends import main as run_main', then calls it. This lets the same script run either locally for quick testing or on a GPU unchanged — confirmed by checking that run_blends.py and register_axiom.py both define a main() invoked exactly this way.

axiomSave.text: After a training run finishes, save_axiom() writes the trained AxiomMLP (a small neural network holding one learned fact, called an 'axiom' in this project) to disk as a .pt file, PyTorch's standard format for saving model weights. This turns an expensive multi-thousand-step training run into something reusable later without retraining from scratch.

axiomVol.text: Like hfCache, this is a second named Modal Volume, 'mimir-axioms', mounted at /axioms and used to permanently store the trained AxiomMLP files produced by axiomSave. Saving here means an axiom trained during one GPU run stays available for future runs, such as the chat server loading it later, long after that GPU container has shut down.

stdoutBuf.text: Since the imported script's main() normally just calls Python's print(), the remote function wraps that call in 'with redirect_stdout(buf):' where buf is an io.StringIO() (an in-memory text buffer) — confirmed at multiple sites in modal_blends.py. This silently captures every line the script would have printed on the remote GPU machine, since ordinary prints there would otherwise be invisible to you locally.

printOut.text: The remote function returns the captured buffer's text via buf.getvalue() as its result, and Modal ships that string back over the network to your laptop as an ordinary Python return value. The local entrypoint then just calls print(output), so all of the GPU script's output appears in your terminal as if it had run locally — this capture-and-ship-back trick is what makes the remote round-trip feel seamless.

nodes.label: Intended vs. lexical paraphrase JSON sets

edges.label: forward pass through explain-prompt + layer hook

paraphrase_data.text: JSON files under data/ (e.g. balance_publisher_paraphrases.json, shoe_town_lexical_paraphrases.json) each hold a list of paraphrase sentences under a 'positives' key. Nearly every probe script loads one or more of these via json.loads(path.read_text())['positives'] as its raw text for both the intended and lexical (dictionary-surface) reading of a term.

model_loader.text: run_injection.py's QwenInjector class wraps AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained plus its tokenizer and installs a permanent forward hook for injection. The Gemma-specific and later probes (probe_gemma_patching.py, probe_layer_contributions.py, probe_natural_vs_extracted.py) instead call AutoModelForCausalLM/AutoTokenizer directly, since Gemma's module layout differs enough that QwenInjector's fixed model.model.layers path doesn't apply.

activation_capture.text: Functions like QwenInjector.hidden_states() (run_injection.py), extract_natural()/extract_axiom() (probe_known_concept.py), capture_residuals()/capture_paraphrase_set() (probe_natural_vs_extracted.py), and position_scan()/extract_at_term() (probe_disambig_locus.py, probe_dual_meaning.py) all run a forward pass with output_hidden_states=True to read out the residual stream (the running sum of vectors flowing layer-to-layer through the transformer) at one chosen layer and token position. Results are usually averaged across a whole paraphrase set into a single representative vector before comparison.

cosine_compare.text: Helpers like probe_natural_vs_extracted.py's cos(), probe_dual_meaning.py's report_cosines(), and probe_known_concept.py's inline checks compute cosine similarity (how closely two vectors point in the same direction, 1.0 = identical, 0 = unrelated) between two captured meaning-vectors. This is how the project checks, for example, whether the model's 'Einstein relativity' reading and its 'cultural relativity' reading actually point in different directions.

logit_lens.text: probe_disambig_locus.py's logit_lens() and probe_natural_vs_extracted.py's top_tokens() both apply the logit lens: projecting an intermediate residual vector through the model's final unembedding matrix to read off which token it currently 'looks like'. That turns an abstract vector into a ranked list of real words a person can actually read.

activation_patching.text: The layer x position sweeps in probe_full_patching_shoe_town.py, probe_full_patching.py, probe_gemma_patching.py, and probe_activation_patching.py all perform activation patching: swapping one prompt's activation into another prompt's forward pass to test its causal effect on the output. probe_layer_contributions.py runs a close cousin, mean-ablation, replacing a layer/position's residual with that layer's own average (instead of another prompt's value) to find which positions causally anchor the lexical reading.

patchscope_decode.text: probe_patchscopes.py's patchscope() implements a Patchscope: decoding a hidden vector's meaning by feeding it into a separate 'explain this' prompt, patching the vector into a placeholder token position, then letting the model generate to see how it verbalizes what's stored there. It reuses capture_concept_completion_residual() from run_logit_bias_decode.py to build the vectors it decodes, even though that file's own main technique — adding a bias vector directly to output logits at every decode step — is a different, decode-time-only mechanism.

console_output.text: Verified: every one of these probe scripts (plus run_logit_bias_decode.py's run_axiom()) only calls print() to report baselines, cosines, logit-lens tokens, and layer/position grids to the terminal; none of them save a plot or write results to disk. The one exception among the related files is run_injection.py's main(), which additionally writes a JSON summary to artifacts/marker_injection_layer{layer}.json alongside its console output.

nodes.label: Injection/Extraction Infra (run_injection, run_extraction, run_n_axiom, trigger_inject, markers, vector_builder, axiom_plan, axiom_classifier, marker_replacement, auto_injector)
